paragraphs.body: It was a hot summer day in the backseat of a Jeep all huddled up with my sister, mom, aunts, and uncle. The drive was long and bumpy, on a deserted road with no clear path ahead. Dust rose in the air as the wheels moved, leaving an orangish cloud behind. The wind coming through the rolled-down windows kept the heat at bay. During the spring season, my mom took us to visit her older sister in Khanaqin, a city in the Garmian region and part of Kurdish territory, to celebrate Nowruz, the festival marking the Kurdish New Year.

My cousins and I all dressed up in traditional Kurdish attire. Draped in various colors of lace, but a distinctive sound could be heard when we moved and danced. Gold bracelets, belts, and necklaces clanged as people swung side by side while holding hands, gradually moved closer to the fire, their jewelry's sound harmonized with the music played in the background. The chirping of birds and the laughter of children fill the air in the distance. At night, torches are lit up and we ascend the mountains, illuminating the path like scattered stars along the trail. Once at the top, fireworks would be lit up, blooming in the sky like a meteor shower. The coldness of the night is eased as the warmth of the burning wood engulfs your senses, while you feel the wind on your cheeks, the wood in your chest, and the smell of dirt under your feet and in that moment I feel homesick to a place I have yet to know. 3 Eid Nowruz is a celebration that originated from the old religion, Zoroastrianism, one that is less practiced than it once was. Kurdish people are religion-tolerant, with many practicing various other faiths. But one consistent aspect that has stayed with Kurds regardless of religion, dialect, or country of residence has been our tradition of burning wood. The word itself Nowruz means a new day. A new day to mark the season of blossom and life, Spring.

“Era emaé emrekeiamen?” (Are we Americans?) My parents used to always say that to me. When I said or did something that went against traditional values. If I asked my parents to stay out late, they would say “Era emaé emrekeiamen?” My answer would always be no, even though I am American. I would reply, “No, we are not Americans, but it’s not the 1970s in Iraq, and things are different now.” Although my parents and I speak the same Kurdish dialect, Garmiani, we don’t always understand each other fully. They spoke as two Kurdish children who were Internally Displaced Persons (IDP), while I spoke as someone who is half-American and half-Kurdish. My parents grew up in the Garmiyan region, known for its dry and garm(warm) climate in the countryside hence the name. They were very young when the government displaced them to central Iraq, leaving them to navigate the school system, language barriers, and cultural differences without any prior understanding of Arabic. To make matters worse they were not allowed to speak Kurdish in school and their dialect only form of communication was oral. This displacement made them even more attached to and proud of their heritage. Ironically, I also experienced a form of displacement, growing up in a different country with a new language and culture. My mom taught me Arabic when I was five so I wouldn’t struggle in school, but in the U.S., I had to teach myself English and then teach it to my parents. Reflecting on this, my parents probably did the same for their parents.

To speak Kurdish means knowing: The pain of losing home. 4 To speak English means knowing: Home is where you speak the same language. Seven countries in the world have the word “stan” after them, yet only Kurdistan is not recognized as a country. The word “stan” means “the people of…”, “the land of”, or “the land where one stands” so Kurdistan is the land of Kurds. It’s the land where we have stood for thousands of years yet stand invisible. If there is no one to recognize us, do we exist as a group of people? When anyone asks where I’m from I say Kurdistan and they always answer with either another Stan country or ask if I’m Turkish. I have Arab friends say Kurds are just Arab or Turkish even though we existed way before Arabs even spoke Arabic. We have existed all this time yet we continue to search for our label as a county. The zâou (land) speaks of my people, the çewîl (mountains) know of our names, the asûman (skies) have heard our songs, and the dar (wood) has heard our prayers as it burns. If the earth, the scripts, and the thousand of history speak of us, who is there to say we are not a country?
To be a kurd is to know: Kurd huç heval naren bes çewîl. To be an American is to know: Kurds have no friends but the mountains.(Boochani)

Even though the Kurds are stateless, my parents always told us to say we were Kurds first, then name our country. My parents wanted my sister and me to take pride in both our Kurdish heritage and our Iraqi birth. My parents are Kurds born in Iraq; I am a Kurd born in Iraq but have grown up in America. Meeting another Kurdish person is quite hard, but meeting one who speaks the same dialect as yours is even harder. The dialects are all Kurdish, yet it’s hard to communicate clearly with someone who speaks Kurmanji as a Garmiani speaker. Kurdish is a language with many roots but communication is hard between them due to the thousands of years of erasure and assimilation that Kurds have been subjected to by neighboring countries. Compared to the widely spoken English language that is used all over the world, Kurdish is a 5 small language that is only spoken in small parts of the globe. You can communicate with almost everyone in the world in English, but it’s like finding a needle in a mountain of hay to find one that speaks home to me. Because Kurds are so spread out regionally and linguistically it creates a sense of us against the other. The bigger dialects assume smaller dialects as not Kurdish enough or simply not Kurdish at all. This is a sentiment I have experienced here in the U.S. from another Kurdish girl who came straight from Kurdistan. I have never been to Kurdistan, but I still take off my shoes before going inside my house, I still eat Tarhana in the winter, have Mashkae from a goat’s stomach, and I still speak Kurdish. Qi ushiden mae chaem? (Who decides what I am?) Era mae Kurdiniem? (why? Am I not Kurdish?)

To speak Garmiani means knowing: the stories of our ancestors. To Speak Arabic means knowing: there are no stories to be told. In my culture, people often live with their parents even after marriage and starting a family. There is a strong cultural emphasis on staying connected with parents and family members in many Kurdish families, placing individual desires in the background. In contrast, American culture has a huge focus on individualism, centering on personal freedom, success, and dreams that do not revolve around family but rather highlight the self as the primary interest. Growing up in a collective culture, my parents would have our neighbors over and send us with plates of food for them. Sharing food is common where I grew up, especially during the holidays. Funerals are another occasion where food is shared with grieving families. "They need to have the energy to grieve," my mom would say. Grieving and celebrating are collective experiences shared by everyone.

In contrast, I experienced a more individualistic society in the West, where the focus is on personal dreams, goals, and desires. My parents always wanted me to pursue a career in 6 pharmaceuticals or engineering, despite my passion for the arts. Exposure to individualism allowed me to seek my personal dreams, instead of prioritizing my parents' goals above mine. To many people who grew up in cultures emphasizing family and community first and viewing elders’ words as gospel, prioritizing one’s own needs is unthinkable.

To live in the East means knowing: There is us, but no Me. To live in the West means knowing: There is me, but no home. When we arrived in Khanaqin, the wind was cool, the sky a clear blue, and the grass greener than I had ever seen. It was midday when we reached my aunt’s house. I still remember the orange and yellow brick walls of her gate. The whole neighborhood spiraled up a hill, with narrow, pebble-strewn roads and greenery everywhere. At the neighborhood entrance, fields stretched out in a beautiful green shade for miles. There was a fireplace with chairs and pillows to the side, and in the distance, soccer goals dotted the field. As the sun set, the sky shifted from blue to shades of yellow and orange, like flames engulfing the wood. The smell of burning wood, fresh grass, and food cooking nearby are so vivid in my memory. I hold that memory close to my dilm (heart). Every spring, I burn a branch of wood and watch it burn at sunset. They say it burns away all that is ill and brings warmth and togetherness. My sister once smelled it and said it made her feel nostalgic for something she couldn’t quite name. Perhaps home and culture are not about what surrounds us but rather the traditions we practice, the words we speak, the connections we have with each other, and our desire to not forget.

“Burning Wood smells like home, red and hot flowing through our veins. Our instinct remembers what our minds have yet to know” Boochani, Behrouz. No Friend But the Mountains: Writing from Manus Prison. Translated by Omid Tofighian, Picador, 2018. Kurds have no friends but the mountains — a proverb echoed in Boochani’s No Friend But the Mountains, symbolizing Kurdish isolation and betrayal.
Commented [1]: probably add some notes about how the words were written and how to say them.

annotations.comment: Gorgeous essay. Beautiful descriptions. The poetic refrains are magic. The interplay between languages is fabulous. It's smart and purposeful and meaningful. Well done.
